speaker: interviewee
text: --early in the morning and stayed there--

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole day?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole day. I don't come out till the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And That's while we find it is faster-- I have a couple of friends with whom we kibitz a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stay around a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we enjoy it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you sit in the shul, or do you go outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only in the shul. No, only in the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: You sit in the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A couple of-- a couple of times we went out for-- you know, just to refresh ourselves, take a little bit of fresh air. But in general, that's the-- that's the whole thing, what we are doing. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I find-- I find the fasting's a lot easier, a lot, lot easier--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you're-- when you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: When you're there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elsewhere. When you're elsewhere, when you're not at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead of coming and going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's no good. No, I don't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yesterday, it was a little bit long. I find it a little bit longer. Because they finished on a quarter to eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't eat till a quarter to eight?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's-- the shul lasted till quarter-- 7:45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: He blew the shofar-- shofar--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shofar and that was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we went out to break-- to break to fast--

speaker: interviewer
text: Break the fast--

speaker: interviewee
text: --with my son and my-- you know, like et cetera, we went out. Well, it was late before-- before we sat down it was 8:30.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's the way it goes. That's how our rabbi likes, the 25-hour business, at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: 25 hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole-- the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole thing, yeah. And I was yesterday, extremely impressed by the rabbi, I-- I would let him know today. For the first time in 20 years, he invited a young girl who just came back from the March of the Living--

speaker: interviewer
text: It is September 24th, 1996. The survivor's name is George Gottlieb. George, G-E-O-R-G-E, Gottlieb, G-O-T-T-L-I-E-B. The interviewer is Joan, J-O-A-N, Karen, K-A-R-E-N, Benbasat, B-E-N-B-A-S-A-T. The city where the interview is taking place is Hallandale in the state of Florida, in the United States of America. The language in which this interview will be taken is English. This is tape one.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is September 24th, 1996. The survivor's name is George Gottlieb. George, G-E-O-R-G-E, Gottlieb, G-O-T-T-L-I-E-B. My name is Joan, J-O-A-N, Karen, K-A-R-E-N, Benbasat, B-E-N-B-A-S-A-T. That city that the interview is being conducted in is Hallandale, in the state of Florida, in the country of the United States of America. This interview will be conducted in English. Please tell me your name and spell it. My name is George Gottlieb, G-E-O-R-G-E. Gottlieb is G-O-T-T-L-I-E-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same name, George Gottlieb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you known by any other name or nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not that I know of. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 16th, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: So today you are, your age?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Budapest, Hungary. Budapest is the capital of Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Imre, I-M-R-E. I'm spelling it right. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Merchant. He-- we had a wholesale general store, grocery, et cetera, everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely close, both to-- I mean, my father was-- I always call it my-- I can't say it, my best friend. Because I had an older brother. But he was extremely close, and very, very instructive, and very-- very-- very much attention was given to us children. And I-- I can't even describe that relationship. I hope I can be as good to my children as he was to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a special interaction between you and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I can tell you that my father was wounded in the First World War. And his left arm was practically useless. And they had a air show. And during that air show I was probably five or six-year-old, maybe seven. I really don't know. And I started to cry, I can't see. I wanted to see the plane. And my father wanted to put me on his shoulder. And as he lifted up-- as he lifted me up on his shoulder, his arm went out. Ambulance had to come. Mom was with my brother. I was with my dad. And the ambulance had to take us to the hospital. And finally, mom found out where we are and-- I couldn't forgive myself that dad, at that time, had to be taken to the hospital. And as a matter of fact, they had to-- that following day, they had to operate on his shoulder. And somehow, somewhere that guilt feeling was. But it showed me the devotion and the love, that even then he couldn't care less. And basically, he comforted us, that don't worry about it. That's part of life, that's all. And his outlook was in generally, always in life, look forwards. And-- and look what tomorrow is going to bring. And everything is going to be all right for you kids and the whole family. That's basically-- that's what completely stands out, as far as my father is concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around six, seven. Cannot be any more. Because I remember as a tiny little-- tiny little thing going, being with dad at the hospital, that was traumatic but-- but lovable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mom's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose. Rose Kohn, born-- named-- I mean, was born as Kohn, but naturally, Rose Gottlieb. Rose spelled R-O-S-E. And Kohn is K-O-H-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gottlieb, what incident best captures the memory of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, probably the worst part of it. Because mom is the one whom I have seen in concentration camp. But that captures, basically, what is unpleasant. And I try to and I would like to forget it. But the best was basically that later I'm going to describe my life. And Friday nights before we get home, and the waiting mother, who was waiting for both for us, basically, with open arms and cuddling, and trying to-- trying to be the real mom. Probably that what-- what's the closest to mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a woman was your mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely surprisingly bold. Both parents, but especially my mom. Today, in 1996, I think about it, that this was in the 1940s, how modern, how advanced, how her whole outlook on life was totally, totally, it went to the point where I don't where-- where-- where she get to the point of being-- requesting-- I would say that it was a daily half-an-hour routine. But we kept, all the time, with mom-- even as little kids-- that a half an hour of reading. And after, we had to tell her what we read about it. Super modern about everything else. I mean, mom was reading. And as matter of fact, when I was around 12, 13, we had to start to read from Huxley to Steinbeck, to all kinds of different American authors, which was completely unknown at that time, especially in Hungary. Some of the books were forbidden, even, to have. And she always tried to-- well, I mean, emphasize basically, what all parents-- and I would like to maybe, I am inserting at the wrong time. But basically, I say that Hitler was able-- Hitler and the Nazis were able to take away everything and anything from my parents to my family, with one exception. And that's what they taught us. That's what we learned. That's what they insisted, that only education can bring you ahead in life. And don't ever give that up. And they were-- mom was just a continuous spark plug and a continuous supporter. And I-- I mean, there is no better description for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Siblings, did you have siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one brother who was 2 and 1/2 years older. His name was Lou, Louis. And he was born June 22, 1926. Which basically puts us approximately 1 and 1/2-- 2 and 1/2 years apart. But only in-- only on the calendar. Because we were extremely, extremely close brothers. A very close family, continuously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else lived in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, my father's father, who was-- once again, typical grandpa in every sense. From spoiling to-- to a certain extent, not only spoiling. The other extent was disciplining. And to him, Judaism-- although my parents had Judaism, we had a kosher home. But to grandpa, Judaism was the-- the teaching. And for us, that was a bribing method to grandpa, continuously, that if you needed at school, anything, or not nice to say, but basically when we wanted to take some money from him, our excuse was always, grandpa, I lost my Bible. And there was no questions asked. But it was given.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was-- he was an older man, but very strict. And I-- I never remember him being sick. I don't-- just don't remember him being sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lipot, L-I-P-O-T. Typical Hungarian name.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk a little about your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I grew up in a small town, basically a suburb of Budapest, which is around 20 miles outside of Budapest. The name of the town is Bicske, B-I-C-S-K-E. We had a nice, beautiful Jewish community with a large synagogue. I would call it on the Orthodox side, American standard. And we grew up basically, on a-- up to the age of 9-- I grew up in-- 10, I grew up in Bicske, where our whole surrounding was there. And mom and dad and grandpa. Grandpa was the president of the synagogue. My father was the president of the school board. And mom was the president of the ladies affiliate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a very-- socially and, and culture-- culturally, we were basically brought up by that time, out of the-- I grew up all my life, as I can remember, being called a dirty Jew. And the little Jew [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And different names. But it meant absolutely-- I mean, at least to us kids, we grew up with it. So it wasn't something what we-- we suffered by it. Naturally, the parents didn't like it. But we grew up to the point that our playground was the-- our school was right next to the synagogue. Right next to it was our playground, where all the Jewish kids, basically, during the week, went to-- went to school. Shabbot we went to-- we played on the playground. And that's, matter of fact, right next to the synagogue and right next to the school. There was a home where the rabbi lived with his mother. He wasn't married. There was another house where the-- our teacher lived with his wife and two children. And the cantor lived immediately right next to it, also with his wife and children. So we had a total supervision or non-supervision, whatever you want to call it. But we lived and they were-- they were our-- not only supervisors, but friends, really, to-- to every extent possible. And at the age of 9, I finished my first schooling-- first schooling process.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's call it-- let's just call it elementary school. And by the European standard at that time, it was a major decision to be. And as far as my parents concerned, there was no question that I'm going to go to the better education system, which is called a gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: And although the Jewish laws did exist by that time, and-- my parents were able to secure-- let's call it secure-- my brother, who was-- as I told you-- 2 and 1/2 years older, was going, already, to an Italian gymnasium, where the total education went in Italian. And Hungarian was only a-- a second language taught twice a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every subject, whatever was at school, was in Italian. I wasn't accepted to any foreign school. And by that time we had the educational laws of anti-Jewish laws.

speaker: interviewee
text: First they started with a-- called numerous clauses, closed numbers for 5% of Jews that are allowed to schooling. By the time I was 10, it was [NON-ENGLISH], which meant zero Jews were allowed to public schooling. Or naturally, that meant universities, etc. And I went to the Jewish gymnasium. And basically, that law was the first law as a child, I understood. I never understood enough. Or as far-- I couldn't care less, let's call it, this way, as a 9, 10-year-old, that my parents had the commercial-- excuse me, commercial anti-Jewish laws or commercial restrictions.

speaker: interviewee
text: That restriction was the first time. And I asked mom and dad the question, so what's going to happen in my life? What's going to happen with me? And what I'm going to be if I cannot go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And mom and dad's continuous reply was don't worry about it. This war cannot last that long. And by that time you are going to be 18, you are going to go onto university, and going to pursue whatever you can and you want to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a kid were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of a kid?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typical, just mischievous little kid. Up till the age 10. And then suddenly realized an awful lot of things started to occur. It started to occur that the freedom is gone. I mean, suddenly you were restricted. And you knew that you had to behave at school. Because there is no other school. There is no way out. There is-- if-- if any-- any time you-- you get into trouble in school, it was major trouble. And especially with what I received from my parents, how important education is, and how important you should get somewhere in life, you knew that you had to do certain things. But school was, at that time, even at age 10-- which is I have three children. I-- I-- I have-- I wasn't able to instill into them, the same thing. But thank goodness, not-- not under the same circumstances. And we understood how important it is. And we were full of sports, beside of school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you, the restrictions of behaving? And what was that like for you, as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Accepted. Accepted and basically knowing-- and-- and it's hard to describe, when you know that even at that age, that as a Jew, you have responsibilities as a Jew. The only way you are going to go somewhere, if you have that education. Without that you are going nowhere. You're going to be a nobody. And you agree. Every Jew, no exception, knew that. And that was part of the Jewish tradition, what we learned from-- from basically you call it here, nursery. But from nursery, kindergarten, then the grades, that's how important it is that you have to-- you have to do certain things. That's your responsibility.

speaker: interviewer
text: What hobbies did you enjoy as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sports. All sports. From soccer to swimming, to skiing to ice hockey, to-- not basketball, very little basketball. That came later, only the gymnasium, basketball. But as a little kid, mostly bicycling. I mean, camping, whatever we could do. Whatever-- whatever life would permit, we were doing it. And basically, I know that camping is something which is not the natural camping, as a child, even at that time, a Jew was not allowed to be a Boy Scout. And not being able to Boy Scout, we overcame by joining or basically, it was officially called the Red Cross Juniors Assembly, I think. So that was the official name. But it was exactly the same-- with the except-- as Boy Scouts. But it was all Jews and only Jews. Nobody else. We were very happy with it. Because we were with our friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the last holiday you spent together with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Honestly, no. But the last-- I mean, it had to be-- the last holiday had to be 19-- my goodness, it's 1943. Rosh Hashanah. Because by-- by Pesach or Passover, we were not together. So it had to be Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and Sukkot. And that's the time when first of all, as-- as I told you, I went to the Jewish gymnasium. So it was automatic holidays. But-- but for my brother to be home, it was absolute. There was no question about it. And we were all together. I mean, it was-- but I really, honestly, don't remember the details.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked a few minutes ago about restrictions with books. How did that affect you as a child? You mentioned previously that some books you were not even permitted to read. How did that affect you, that restriction affect you as a child growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, the basic idea which is when you grow up as a Jewish kid, you know what certain things you are going to be forbidden. The second I would call it Jewish laws, which tremendously affected all of us, were just for the entertainment. It was the entertainment laws. And those laws forbid Jews to go to theaters. I mean, I'm talking not only movie theaters, but regular live theaters, operas, concerts. As kids, at that time, so I think-- so 12 or 13, maybe 11-year-old, when the first time we were not allowed to go to first division soccer games. And that was our national sport. And not only national sport, but as kids, that's what we practiced the most. We are not allowed to go to the first-- first-class tennis games, or any one of the entertainment, whatsoever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to go to second division, or you know, not to have to-- the national theater, the national theaters, to the theater. Not to be able to go to the opera house, what my mom and dad loved. And we used to go with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very interestingly, I can tell you that I distinctly remember that dad didn't like ballet. Mom loved ballet. And mom dragged us to the ballet, the both boys. And we-- I would say that we didn't like it, mostly because dad didn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, most probably, the reaction of-- of-- normal reaction of boys. But at the same time, mom was a good sport and used to come with us to soccer game. So suddenly, that was taken away from us. You know, because she wouldn't come to the-- and even dad wouldn't come to the local little soccer game. Or what we are-- it was something what we missed, something what you realize that it was taken away. And it's part of culture. Out of prideness, and kind of-- What you have to understand, that as much as we were prosecuted as children, we grew up as Hungarians. Our religion was Jewish, but we were Hungarians. I distinctly remember the-- I mean, I have been only seven-year-old-- in 1936, Olympic-- Olympic games in Berlin. And a Hungarian swimmer won a gold medal. We were-- gold medal. And we were the super proudest people on earth. As a Hungarian was able to win. We knew about Jesse Owens. And we were very proud. He was an American. And basically, he defeated Hitler right in his backyard. But our main happiness was the same way as it was an international soccer game, we rooted for Hungary, and never even thought anything else. We were Hungarians. But and we-- we found it extremely difficult, to a certain extent, especially during conversation by parents. What I have to add is I had the most unbelievable parents, if I think about it today, that are that age already. The normal thing was that at the time when we set down-- which was very strict-- what time we had to be home, and what time was supper. And unknown to North American, even-- even-- I would say that even for my children, what the rules and regulations that nobody could sit down at the table before my grandfather sat down, not even my father. Then it was my father. Then we could sit down. I mean, that was the normal routine. And mom was taking care of everybody. But although we had helper in the house. But that was the normal routine. And nobody could stand up before grandpa stood up. And during that magic time of whatever was the time, half an hour, whatever was the time, there was the most open discussion. What-- I don't know if anybody-- I talked to many friends. And I had many, many friends. And all those friends, they never had that. They never had the openness. They never had the-- that kind of family discussions what are-- what was from politics to family, to-- to our lives, to our children's lives, to-- to parents' life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was wide, wide open. And wide, wide-- that's the reason I said before, that the modern feeling, I think it was just unbelievable. I don't know how-- how else can I say it? But today I know how much I appreciate it. You spoke before about being taunted because you were Jewish. Tell me a little about the non-Jews' relationship to the Jews, where you lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that you had to understand that we were called a dirty Jew. They-- we were totally separate group. And I have to use the word group. We learned, as a kid, that you don't go alone. You don't go, even, on a-- on your bicycle in front of your house to the next house or the third house alone. You go with your brother. We go with your friends. You go-- you go to synagogue together, to school together. And not only the taunt thing. But sometimes it was a beating. And sometimes it was a fight. We learned quite early that you don't let yourself. And you don't-- don't be the coward. And even if they are six and you are three, you fight back. Not only fight back, but-- but if you fight back strong, the next time they are not going to dare to come and do the same thing. And so they-- they threw a stone, so you threw it back. You-- they called you a name, you didn't have to call any name because they had no name. I mean, we were never taught to call somebody somebody else because of their different religion. They called us a dirty Jew. Good, I was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe what we said, is I washed. Did you wash? I'm clean. In your community was there segregation? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no such thing as a real segregation. I mean, we were completely interwoven in-- in Bicske, which is completely 16,000, 18,000 people, we had 400 Jewish families. So it was not a small-- just we couldn't be segregated. And you know, like our school was basically a school. I mean, our synagogue was a big synagogue. I mean, excuse me. It wasn't 400 families, 400 people. That's a big difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you become aware of changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: As long as I can remember. Really, as long as I can remember. [INAUDIBLE]. I don't believe that I am-- I don't believe that I remember more than the age of four, five, six. And four, even no-- I think it was five and six. That was the time when I remember taking-- taking grandpa in the morning, to the synagogue. And going to pick him up, going with him to the synagogue. That's the only time-- that's the earliest time, let's say, what I remember. And by that time we were-- we were called a dirty Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop for one moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything all right?
